nativeRange.habitat: Permanently submerged in still and slow-moving freshwater pools, streams, and shallow ponds in central and northern Madagascar; tolerates both sunny and partially shaded conditions, with seasonal water-level fluctuations in some habitats.

decorPreferences: nutrient-rich substrate (aquasoil) or fine gravel/sand with root tabs
bulb planted on top of or only half-buried in the substrate to prevent rot
open background area with at least 50-60 cm of vertical clearance for ruffled leaves
moderate to bright lighting with stable, soft to moderately hard water

goodWith: small peaceful cichlids such as rams
avoidWith: plant-eating fish such as silver dollars or buenos aires tetras

summary: Madagascar-endemic bulb plant prized for its large, translucent, ruffled bright-green leaves. Generally considered an easy showpiece background plant for medium to large tanks.
careNotes: Aponogeton ulvaceus is a bulb plant endemic to central and northern Madagascar, valued for its large, semi-translucent, wavy-edged or ruffled leaves that tend to range from bright green to pale bronze depending on light intensity. Leaves commonly reach 30-50 cm and can exceed 50 cm under strong lighting, so the species is generally placed in the background of tanks of around 150 L or larger with a footprint of about 90 cm and roughly 50-80 cm of vertical clearance. A single healthy bulb has been reported to produce up to forty leaves in good conditions, creating a dramatic showpiece. It is commonly considered beginner-friendly and tolerant of a fairly broad range of conditions — temperatures around 22-28 °C, pH roughly 6.5-7.5, and soft to moderately hard water (about 2-12 dGH). CO2 injection is not required but tends to boost size and growth speed when combined with a nutrient-rich substrate, root tabs, or regular liquid fertilizer. The bulb is best planted on top of, or only half-buried in, the substrate to avoid rot. Like other Aponogetons, this species commonly enters a natural dormancy where leaves die back for several weeks to months; do NOT discard the bulb — leave it in the substrate (or store it briefly in damp sand in cool, dark conditions) and new leaves typically resprout once the rest period ends. Note that one researched source (Modern Aquarium) listed the origin as Sri Lanka, which conflicts with Wikipedia, The Aquarium Guide, and the wider literature placing it in Madagascar; Madagascar has been used here.
breedingNotes: Propagates primarily by flowering — flower spikes emerge above the water surface and can self-pollinate or be hand-pollinated by gently rubbing two blooms together, producing seeds that can be grown on separately. Daughter bulbs may also form on mature plants and can be separated and replanted.

primary.name: Modern Aquarium
primary.notes: Promoted to primary after the manifest-supplied Buce Plant URL (https://buceplant.com/products/aponogeton-ulvaceus-bulb) returned HTTP 404. Provided lighting, temperature (70-82 F = 21-28 C), placement, substrate, fertilization, and dormancy cycle guidance. NOTE: this source incorrectly states origin as Sri Lanka; Madagascar origin used here per Wikipedia, The Aquarium Guide, and broader taxonomic literature.

additional.name: Wikipedia (Aponogeton ulvaceus)
additional.notes: Authoritative source for taxonomy (Aponogetonaceae, Alismatales), native range (central and northern Madagascar), leaf morphology (>50 cm long, up to 40 leaves per bulb, pale green/reddish under intense light, wavy translucent), and dormancy behaviour.

additional.name: The Aquarium Guide
additional.notes: Cross-checked Madagascar origin, height 30-50 cm, fast growth, temperature 22-28 C, pH 6.5-7.5, midground/background placement, beginner care level, dormancy procedure (cool damp sand for ~2 months), propagation via seeds and runners.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op
additional.notes: Confirmed easy care level, fast growth, large translucent wavy bright-green leaves, and shelter value for fish/shrimp/fry. Page is light on numeric parameters.
